primer.markdown:
# The three things every category has

A category is specified by three pieces of data: **objects**, **morphisms** (arrows) between objects, and a **composition** operation that combines two compatible arrows into a third. Add two laws — identity (every object has an arrow to itself that does nothing) and associativity (`(h ∘ g) ∘ f = h ∘ (g ∘ f)`) — and you have a category.

The `CATEGORICAL-ONTOLOGY.md` document in `categorical-meta-prompting` fixes the ontology the rest of the framework depends on: the **objects** of the meta-prompting category are `Task`, `Prompt`, `Context`, and `Quality ∈ [0,1]`; the **morphisms** are the composition operators `→` (sequence), `||` (parallel), `⊗` (tensor), and `>=>` (Kleisli); and composition is explicitly tracked through a quality lattice where `quality(A ⊗ B) ≤ min(q(A), q(B))` and `quality(A || B) = mean(q(A), q(B))`.

Why this matters: once you agree that prompts live in a category, you are no longer stringing together strings. You are composing typed arrows, and the composition laws — identity and associativity — give you predictable behaviour. `F(id_task) = id_prompt` means wrapping a no-op task yields a no-op prompt; `F(g ∘ f) = F(g) ∘ F(f)` means composing two task transformations equals transforming each separately and composing the results. Those two lines are the promise that categorical meta-prompting makes to you, and everything else in this Quest is earning it.
primer.key_insight: Objects, morphisms, and composition — plus identity and associativity — are the minimal vocabulary every category requires. Meta-prompting was missing exactly that vocabulary.

visual.alt: Diagram of the four canonical objects (Task, Prompt, Context, Quality) arranged as nodes, with arrows labelled by the four composition operators (→ sequence, || parallel, ⊗ tensor, >=> Kleisli) showing how morphisms flow between them. Identity loops at each node illustrate the identity law; an associativity triangle in the corner shows (h ∘ g) ∘ f = h ∘ (g ∘ f).

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner selects two morphisms f: Task → Prompt and g: Prompt → Context, clicks 'compose', and the widget renders g ∘ f: Task → Context along with the quality rule min(q_f, q_g). Identity morphism is then applied and the widget shows that id ∘ f = f (left identity) — confirming the identity law.

artifact.excerpt:
LAWS:
  • F(id_task) = id_prompt           (identity preserved)
  • F(g ∘ f) = F(g) ∘ F(f)           (composition preserved)

COMPOSITION OPERATORS AS MORPHISMS
  → (SEQUENCE)      Quality: min(qₐ, qᵦ)
  || (PARALLEL)     Quality: mean(qᵦ, qᵧ, qᵨ)
  ⊗ (TENSOR)        Quality: min(qₐ, qᵦ)
  >=> (KLEISLI)     Quality: converges iteratively

questions.prompt: A category is specified by objects, morphisms, and composition. Which two laws must composition satisfy for the specification to actually be a category?
questions.explanation: Every category requires (a) identity — for each object X there is an identity morphism id_X such that id_X ∘ f = f and g ∘ id_X = g for any compatible f, g — and (b) associativity: (h ∘ g) ∘ f = h ∘ (g ∘ f). These are the two axioms stated in `docs/CATEGORICAL-ONTOLOGY.md §2.1` and enforced by the framework as `F(id) = id` and `F(g ∘ f) = F(g) ∘ F(f)`.

questions.prompt: In the categorical-meta-prompting ontology, the → (sequence) operator and the ⊗ (tensor) operator share the same quality rule. What is it?
questions.explanation: Both use `min(q₁, q₂)`. `docs/CATEGORICAL-ONTOLOGY.md §3.1` states: `→ (SEQUENCE) Quality: min(qₐ, qᵦ)` and `⊗ (TENSOR) Quality: min(qₐ, qᵦ)`. The intuition: a chain or a tensor is only as good as its weakest link. Parallel (`||`) is the one that averages; Kleisli (`>=>`) is the one that converges iteratively.

questions.prompt: Why does the framework choose objects like `Task`, `Prompt`, `Context`, `Quality` rather than just `String`?
questions.explanation: Because categories only work when morphisms are typed. If every object is `String`, then every arrow is `String → String` and composition is trivial but meaningless. Distinct object types (`Task`, `Prompt`, `Context`) make `F: Task → Prompt` a real structural claim, not a rename — and they are what let the identity and composition laws carry content.
